mnemonics.title: FTP Commands
mnemonics.content: FTP Command Mnemonic: 'Please Don't Throw Sausage Pizza Away' for: PWD (Print Working Directory), DIR (List files), TYPE (Set transfer type), STOR (Store/upload), PASV (Passive mode), APPE (Append).

mnemonics.title: HTTP Status Codes
mnemonics.content: HTTP Status: '200 OK, 300 Multiple Choices, 400 Bad Client, 500 Server Trouble' - First digit indicates class: 1xx=Info, 2xx=Success, 3xx=Redirection, 4xx=Client Error, 5xx=Server Error.

mnemonics.title: SMTP Commands
mnemonics.content: SMTP Sequence: 'HELO introduces, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO tells who, DATA sends the message, QUIT ends' - Remember the order for sending emails.

acronyms.usage: Used for uploading/downloading files between client and server.
Protocol for sending emails between mail servers.
Protocol for transferring web pages and data on the World Wide Web.
Secure version of HTTP using TLS/SSL encryption.
Address used to locate resources on the web (e.g., https://www.example.com).
Language used to create web pages.
Protocol for remote terminal access to network devices.
Email client like Outlook, Thunderbird that composes and sends emails.
Server that transfers emails between servers (e.g., Postfix, Sendmail).

visualTips.visualization: Think of a restaurant: Client (customer) places order (GET/POST), Kitchen (server) processes it, Server brings food (Response with status code + data). HTTP is the waiter delivering orders.
Active mode = Client opens door for server to deliver data. Passive mode = Client walks to server's room to pick up data. Both get the file, but connection method differs.
Think of traditional mail: MUA is you writing letter, MTA is post office sorting and sending, MDA is letter carrier delivering to recipient's mailbox.
TCP/IP is the highway system (infrastructure). HTTP is the trucks (vehicles) carrying specific cargo (web content) on those highways.

analogies.analogy: HTTP is like a postcard - anyone can read the message. HTTPS is like a sealed envelope - only sender and receiver can read it.
Each HTTP request is like calling a customer service center where the representative doesn't remember your previous calls - you must identify yourself (cookies/session) each time.
FTP control connection is like a telephone line for giving commands, while data connection is like a separate delivery truck for actually moving files.
Web browser is like a customer in a restaurant (placing orders), and web server is like the kitchen (preparing and serving food). The menu (HTML) tells what's available.
